statement.markdown: Given the root of a binary tree, return the zigzag level order traversal of its nodes' values. (i.e., from left to right, then right to left for the next level and alternate between).
statement.inputFormat:
1
<root node value>
2
<left child node value>
<right child node value>
... (repeat for each node, with -1 for null children)
statement.outputFormat:
<level 1 values, space-separated>
<level 2 values, space-separated, in reverse order>
...
<level n values, space-separated, following zigzag pattern>
statement.notes: The input tree nodes have the following structure: { val: int, left: TreeNode, right: TreeNode }

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the tree.

examples.explanation: The zigzag level order traversal of the tree is: level 1: 1, level 2: 3 2.
The zigzag level order traversal of the tree is: level 1: 1, level 2: 3 2, level 3: 4 5.

hints.text: The problem requires traversing a binary tree level by level but with a twist: the direction of traversal alternates between levels.
A typical approach to traverse a tree level by level is to use a queue data structure to keep track of nodes at each level.
To incorporate the zigzag pattern, consider using an additional data structure or a flag to track the direction of traversal at each level, and adjust the node processing order accordingly.
The key to solving this problem efficiently is to realize that you can reverse the order of node values at every other level to achieve the zigzag effect.

editorial.title: Zigzag Spiral Traversal of a Binary Tree
editorial.intuition: The problem requires traversing a binary tree in a zigzag or spiral pattern, which means alternating the direction of traversal at each level. A brute force approach would involve traversing the tree level by level and storing the node values, then reversing every other level. However, this approach is slow for large trees because it requires extra space to store the node values and extra time to reverse the levels.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would be to use a level order traversal (BFS) algorithm to traverse the tree, storing the node values at each level in a data structure such as a list or queue. Then, iterate over the levels, reversing every other level to achieve the zigzag pattern. This approach has a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(n), where n is the number of nodes in the tree.
editorial.betterSolution: The key observation that unlocks the optimal approach is that we don't need to store all the node values and then reverse every other level. Instead, we can traverse the tree level by level, and for every other level, reverse the node values on the fly.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses a level order traversal (BFS) algorithm with a queue data structure to traverse the tree. At each level, store the node values in a list, and if the level is odd, reverse the list before adding it to the result. This approach also has a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(n), but it avoids the extra space required to store the node values and the extra time required to reverse the levels.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proved by induction on the levels of the tree. The base case is the root level, which is trivially correct. For the inductive step, assume that the solution is correct for all levels up to k. Then, for level k+1, the solution either adds the node values in the correct order (if k+1 is even) or reverses them (if k+1 is odd), which maintains the zigzag pattern.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the optimal solution on a sample binary tree, such as a tree with 5 nodes (1, 2, 3, 4, 5), verifies that the solution produces the correct zigzag spiral traversal: [[1], [3, 2], [4, 5]].
commonMistakes: Not handling the case where a node has only one child.
Not reversing the node values for odd levels.
Using a recursive approach instead of an iterative one.
Not checking for null or -1 node values.
Not using a queue data structure to traverse the tree level by level.
Not handling the case where the tree has only one node.
